# Alfred Lin: How to Think (and Work) Like a World Class Investor [The Knowledge Project Ep. #212]

Alfred Lin shares strategies for navigating startup challenges, building resilient teams, and creating long-lasting value. Lin explores lessons from companies like Zappos, Airbnb, DoorDash, and Amazon, offering actionable insights on topics like hiring for potential, managing crises, and fostering innovative cultures. Learn how first-principles thinking, customer focus, and disciplined growth can transform challenges into opportunities, even in the face of unprecedented disruptions.

**Available now: [YouTube](https://youtu.be/91WKEsbHUNc) | [Apple Podcasts](https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/212-alfred-lin-how-to-think-and-work-like-a-world/id990149481?i=1000684831772) | [Spotify](https://open.spotify.com/episode/4GnfUY4G1pHjV50fxbQs5f?si=1ac9b2dfc8eb48a2) | [Transcript](https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/alfred-lin-212/)**

Lin is a partner at Sequoia Capital and represents Sequoia on boards like Airbnb and DoorDash. From January 2005 to December 2010, he served as Chairman of the Board and Chief Operating Officer of Zappos. He has a Bachelor’s in Applied Mathematics from Harvard and a Master’s in Statistics from Stanford.

### About Alfred Lin

Alfred Lin is a three-time #1 Midas List investor at Sequoia Capital who brings an unusual operator's brain to venture investing, having previously built Zappos as COO, CFO, and Chairman alongside Tony Hsieh, which gives him a hands-on approach to helping founders with detailed operational questions rather than just board-level strategy. He's known for his exceptionally fast ability to reframe markets and recalculate opportunities in a single meeting, often grasping new categories more deeply than people who have studied them for months, and he specifically seeks outsider founders who are driven by personal pain to correct something the world got wrong rather than those with conventional domain experience.

### Sequoia Capital

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62f3f3ccd0cfd515f3b095e4/6407cdb682be2b43f32d9464_CB%20Dark.svg)

Sequoia Capital pioneered a "market-first" investing philosophy focused on targeting big markets rather than just picking the best people, which they've industrialized into a repeatable company-building playbook with structured founder support programs like Company Design and Arc. The firm broke from traditional venture capital by creating an evergreen fund structure in 2021 and operating a global Scout Program that has quietly backed over 1,000 startups through a network of seeded operators and founders.

When [Alfred Lin](https://sequoiacap.com/people/alfred-lin/) joined Sequoia Capital roughly 15 years ago, the largest technology companies in the world carried market capitalizations of a few hundred billion dollars. Earlier today, the newly appointed co-steward of one of Silicon Valley's most storied venture firms [published a detailed framework on X](https://x.com/Alfred_Lin/status/2033933306690261037) arguing that the entire venture industry needs to revise what a successful outcome looks like, pointing to Nvidia's ascent past $5 trillion as the clearest evidence yet that the conventional mental model of company value is structurally obsolete.

The post, which drew more than 64,000 views within hours of publication, was timed to coincide with the opening of [Nvidia's annual GTC developer conference](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/gtc/), where CEO Jensen Huang presented what he described as an unprecedented revenue pipeline. Lin, who co-hosted a pre-event session alongside Sarah Guo, used the occasion to lay out a reductionist investment framework built around four variables: the size of the prize, the probability of success, the total investment cost, and the time to exit.

## Four Variables, One Persistent Blind Spot

Lin's framework identifies investment cost estimation as the area where practitioners are most reliable. Probability of success, he writes, is systematically overstated, with more experienced investors tending toward greater optimism. Time horizons beyond one year are routinely underestimated. But the variable where the industry is most consistently, and most consequentially, wrong is the size of the prize.

The conventional explanation for this misjudgment is a failure of imagination. Investors, the argument goes, simply could not picture a world in which companies like Airbnb, DoorDash, or Uber would be worth what they are today. Lin acknowledges that argument but pushes further. The deeper cause, he contends, is the systematic underappreciation of consistent compounding over multi-decade time horizons. A handful of companies that were already valued in the hundreds of billions, compounding at 10 percent annually for 15 years, would mechanically arrive at several trillion dollars in market capitalization. The math, Lin notes, is not exotic.

## Nvidia's Milestone Trajectory

The specific data point anchoring Lin's argument is Nvidia's market cap trajectory. According to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2025/10/29/nvidia-first-5-trillion-company-ceo-jensen-huang-500-billion-revenue-blackwell-rubin-gpus-china/), the chipmaker crossed $1 trillion in June 2023, $2 trillion in February 2024, and $3 trillion in June 2024. Lin's timeline on X adds that Nvidia crossed $4 trillion in July 2025 before [becoming the first company in history to breach $5 trillion in market capitalization on October 29, 2025](https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/29/nvidia-becomes-first-public-company-worth-5-trillion/). The company had been valued at approximately $400 billion before the public debut of OpenAI's ChatGPT in late 2022.

MORE FOR YOU

That trajectory, condensed: it took Nvidia three decades to reach $1 trillion and just over two years to add the next $4 trillion. The milestone was driven in large part by the company's dominance in AI accelerators. [CNBC reported](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/10/29/nvidia-on-track-to-hit-historic-5-trillion-valuation-amid-ai-rally.html) that Huang disclosed at the GTC Washington conference that Nvidia had secured more than $500 billion in orders for its Blackwell and Rubin AI chips through the end of 2026. [TechCrunch noted](https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/29/nvidia-becomes-first-public-company-worth-5-trillion/) that the company controls an estimated 90 percent of the market for AI chips used in data center training and inference, supplying Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, OpenAI, and others.

Lin uses Nvidia's arc to frame a broader probabilistic argument: if the company were to compound at just 10 percent annually for another decade from its current $5 trillion base, it would cross $10 trillion. He is not issuing a forecast. The figure is illustrative of how compounding, applied to already-massive bases, produces outcomes that strain prior mental models of scale.

## A New Taxonomy of Outcomes

In a [recent conversation with Sourcery](https://www.sourcery.vc/p/breaking-alfred-lin-the-future-according), Lin outlined how the change in scale has already altered Sequoia's internal benchmarks. When he joined the firm, an investment had to generate $100 million in returns to be recognized on the firm's wall of legendary outcomes. That figure has since increased to well over $1 billion, and Lin expects the threshold to continue rising. In the same conversation, he noted that when he joined Sequoia the largest market cap companies were in the $300 to $400 billion range, and that companies worth $4 to $5 trillion are now the comparable reference point.

In the X post, Lin maps out a generational compounding arc for outlier founding teams: in a decade, a seed-stage company could become worth $1 billion to $10 billion; in two decades, the same company might reach $10 billion to $100 billion; in three decades, $100 billion to $1 trillion; and in four decades, $1 trillion to $10 trillion. This is the architecture of the venture business, he argues, but it requires patience measured in decades, not fund cycles. In response to a reply from [Molly O'Shea](https://x.com/MollySOShea) summarizing his argument, Lin offered a naming convention for the next tier of milestone companies: "$1T = Teracorn? $10T = Titan?"

## A Structural Challenge For The VC Industry

Standard fund structures are built around a ten-year life, creating distribution pressure that cuts against holding the most compounding assets for the three or four decades the framework envisions. [A March 2026 analysis by Invezz](https://invezz.com/news/2026/03/12/vc-investors-200-billion-hole-where-did-all-the-money-go/) found that more than 1,500 unicorns collectively worth $6 trillion have no clear path to exit following years of frozen IPO markets, with distributions to limited partners falling to roughly 6 percent of assets under management in the first half of 2025, less than half the ten-year historical average.

At the same time, capital concentration has intensified. According to an [OECD policy brief published in January 2026](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/venture-capital-investments-in-artificial-intelligence-through-2025_a13752f5-en/full-report.html), AI firms captured 61 percent of all global venture capital in 2025, totaling $258.7 billion of $427.1 billion deployed. A [Cambridge Associates 2026 outlook](https://www.cambridgeassociates.com/insight/2026-outlook-private-equity-venture-capital-views/) noted that continuation vehicles are expected to represent at least 20 percent of distributions in 2026 as limited partners seek liquidity through secondary markets, a workaround that reflects the mismatch between fund timelines and the actual arc of compounding returns.

The concentration dynamic runs even deeper. [SVB's 2026 State of the Markets report](https://www.saastr.com/340-billion-in-vc-but-fewer-deals-than-any-year-this-decade-10-things/) found that in 2025, nearly $340 billion flowed into U.S. venture-backed companies, but the top 1 percent of companies by valuation captured a full third of all capital, while the bottom half received just 7 percent. The number of individual deals declined 15 percent year over year even as total dollars deployed rose 53 percent.

## Running The Distance

The second half of Lin’s thesis is behavioral. Updating the size of the prize is necessary but insufficient. The harder task is recalibrating what it means to remain committed to a company or investment over four decades, through multiple technology waves and against the constant pull of near-term performance pressure. Lin has articulated a version of this philosophy in other contexts, including [a Harvard Innovation Labs profile](https://innovationlabs.harvard.edu/articles/alfred-lin) in which he described his personal framework for sustained improvement as becoming one percent better each day. At annual compounding, that yields a 37.7x improvement over a year. The same arithmetic logic that makes Nvidia's trajectory legible in hindsight makes it predictable in structure, if not in outcome, in advance.

[A Fortune interview from March 2026](https://fortune.com/2026/03/02/alfred-lin-sequoia-saas-ai-citrini-research-vertical-software-rowspace/) found Lin continuing to push back against narratives that AI foundation models will render software businesses obsolete, arguing that durable companies are built on compounding customer value, not on any single technological layer. Since 2020, Sequoia has distributed more than $43 billion back to its limited partners, positioning the firm as what Lin describes as a net liquidity provider rather than a capital accumulator.

The core argument in Lin's post amounts to a recalibration memo for an industry that has spent decades systematically underpricing the far end of the probability distribution. The math of compounding is not new. What is new is that several companies have now lived long enough, and grown fast enough, to make the output of that math visible in real time. Lin's contention is that investors who fail to update their priors accordingly are not being conservative. They are, in the original sense of the word he uses, underestimating the prize.

## Last Updated May 27, 2026, 6:30am EDT

* Since joining Sequoia in 2010, Alfred Lin has spearheaded some of the venture firm's biggest investments, including DoorDash and Airbnb. He is the co-captain of Sequoia Capital's early-stage investment business alongside Luciana Lixandru.
* Lin, who returns to the Midas List for a 14th time this year, became a "co-steward" of Sequoia Capital with Pat Grady after Roelof Botha stepped down in November 2025.
* Lin co-led Sequoia's 2021 investment in OpenAI, valued at $852 billion as of March. He also led Sequoia's Series B investment in Reddit. In March 2024, Reddit went public in a $6.5 billion IPO.
* Lin holds a board seat at Citadel Securities, which sold a $1.15 billion stake in its company to Sequoia and Paradigm in January 2022.
* Prior to joining Sequoia in 2010, Lin helped launch the online retailer Zappos with his Harvard classmate Tony Hsieh. He served as the company's COO and CFO. In July 2009, Amazon acquired Zappos.

# Lessons from Alfred Lin

Alfred Lin, a partner at Sequoia Capital and a key architect behind the success of companies like Zappos, Airbnb, and DoorDash, is a revered figure in Silicon Valley. His journey from an immigrant from Taiwan to a top-ranking venture capitalist has been marked by a distinct investment philosophy and a deep understanding of what it takes to build legendary companies.

### **On Company Culture and Leadership**

Lin, heavily influenced by his time as COO and CFO of Zappos, is a staunch advocate for the power of a strong and intentional company culture.

1. **On the foundation of culture:** "You have to start with the leader of the company and the founder, and ask yourself: What are the values that are the most important to you?" [[1]](https://cub.club/blog/building-a-strong-workplace-culture-7-tips-from-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
2. **On defining values:** A helpful exercise is to think about the people you never enjoyed working with, identify the worst things about working with them, and do the opposite. [[1]](https://cub.club/blog/building-a-strong-workplace-culture-7-tips-from-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
3. **On the purpose of culture:** "A strong culture with core values can align people, provide stability and trust, and help retain the right employees." [[2]](https://learnwithtree.com/blogs/y-combinator-how-to-start-a-startup/yc-lecture-10-culture?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
4. **On hiring for cultural fit:** "I think you can have the smartest engineer in the world, but if they don't believe the mission they are not going to pour their heart and soul into it." [[1]](https://cub.club/blog/building-a-strong-workplace-culture-7-tips-from-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com) It's easier to teach skills than to instill values. [[1]](https://cub.club/blog/building-a-strong-workplace-culture-7-tips-from-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
5. **On the nature of culture:** "There's no such thing as a good or bad culture, it's either a strong or weak culture. And a good culture for somebody else may not be a good culture for you." [[3]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RfWgVWGEuGE&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
6. **On servant leadership:** "My leadership style is not complicated... My job is to make other people successful. I want others to have the best track record that they can. I fundamentally believe in servant leadership." [[4]](https://tylerhogge.com/2024/01/18/what-i-learned-from-alfred-lin-of-sequoia/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
7. **On daily reinforcement:** "Everybody wants to provide great customer service, every company wants to have great culture. What they fail to do is make it a daily habit." [[1]](https://cub.club/blog/building-a-strong-workplace-culture-7-tips-from-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
8. **On cultural homogeneity:** While diversity in background and beliefs is crucial, you want homogeneity in values. [[3]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RfWgVWGEuGE&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
9. **On Zappos' core value:** "We are very specific that we wanted to deliver great customer service and it was going to be a 'wow' experience." [[1]](https://cub.club/blog/building-a-strong-workplace-culture-7-tips-from-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
10. **On the evolution of a company:** Founders must transition from building a product to building the company that builds the product. [[3]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RfWgVWGEuGE&ref=antoinebuteau.com)

### **For Founders and Entrepreneurs**

Lin's advice for founders is both pragmatic and aspirational, grounded in his extensive experience as both an operator and an investor.

1. **On the founder's mindset:** "The toughest job of a founder is holding two opposite ideas in your head and still function." [[5]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PDAcJ-ih4wA&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
2. **On having a unique insight:** "If you're starting something, I think you need to have a unique insight into what the world has gotten wrong and why you have a unique ability to solve that problem." [[6]](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=SV0KFc4cSxc&pp=ygUSI2NocmlzdGluYWNhY2lvcHBv&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
3. **On the entrepreneurial journey:** "The entrepreneurial journey is long and hard. It is also lonely, because you can't necessarily share your struggles." [[7]](https://thoughteconomics.com/sequoia-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
4. **On constant self-reinvention:** "You're going to have to constantly re-create yourself as a founder. You are under no obligation to remain the same person." [[4]](https://tylerhogge.com/2024/01/18/what-i-learned-from-alfred-lin-of-sequoia/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
5. **On passion and market size:** Founders should have a passion for the business problem they are solving and then determine if the market is large enough, rather than starting with large markets and trying to find a passion. [[6]](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=SV0KFc4cSxc&pp=ygUSI2NocmlzdGluYWNhY2lvcHBv&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
6. **On the importance of "Why now?":** Early-stage investing is predicated on investing in markets undergoing significant change. [[8]](https://www.acquired.fm/episodes/special-sequoia-capitals-investment-playbook-with-alfred-lin?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
7. **On optimism and vision:** "Successful companies have really optimistic founders... Great companies have this great vision of the world that they paint." [[6]](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=SV0KFc4cSxc&pp=ygUSI2NocmlzdGluYWNhY2lvcHBv&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
8. **On building a sustainable company first:** When asked for advice on getting a company sold, Lin's simple answer is to "build a real sustainable company first." [[9]](https://vator.tv/2010-04-20-lessons-on-building-zappos-to-an-exit/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
9. **On iteration:** "As a startup you don't necessarily know [the right business model]. To that end, find one that's at least a big opportunity and try, iterate, try, iterate, etc." [[9]](https://vator.tv/2010-04-20-lessons-on-building-zappos-to-an-exit/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
10. **On competition:** "The best companies never talk about the competition. They just talk about the fact that they're different than everybody else." [[10]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-_QgYmRpCQ0&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
11. **On having your head in the clouds and feet on the ground:** Lin quotes Richard Feynman's description of Albert Einstein, applying it to founders: "He has his head in the clouds, but his feet on the ground." [[5]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PDAcJ-ih4wA&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
12. **On defensibility:** "No startup is defensible on day one. Your job is to build something that the world has never seen before and then run as fast as possible in the right direction." [[5]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PDAcJ-ih4wA&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
13. **On the power of compounding:** "At Zappos, we preached the power of improving one percent every day... If you start off with $1 at the beginning of the year, and you can compound it one percent daily, you will have more than $37 at the end of the year." [[11]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/article/seven-questions-with-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
14. **On making mistakes:** "Making mistakes isn't the sin. The sin is not being willing to admit and correct those mistakes." [[11]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/article/seven-questions-with-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)

### **On Investing and Venture Capital**

As a partner at Sequoia, Lin's investment philosophy is centered on long-term partnerships and identifying outlier founders.

1. **The goal at Sequoia:** "We help the daring build legendary companies." [[7]](https://thoughteconomics.com/sequoia-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
2. **What investors look for:** All investors seek outlier founders, a delightful product, a massive market, and a disruptive business model. [[12]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/people/alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
3. **The Sequoia edge:** "To have an edge, you have to play the game differently. I am much more focused on the founders' special characteristics, their novel and unique insights, the eureka moment of their founding story, and their lived experiences that creates empathy for the customer they will serve." [[12]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/people/alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
4. **On founder-market fit:** "Basically, I am on the lookout for founder-market fit." [[12]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/people/alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
5. **The ultimate criterion:** "After you strip through all that, there is only one criteria for me: would I love to work for the founders and help them reach their and their company's full potential?" [[12]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/people/alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
6. **On being partners, not just investors:** "We don't buy low, we don't buy and then we don't sell high. Our job is to become partners with the founders." [[13]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rbksDmp4k9M&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
7. **On long-term obsession:** "A key part of our culture is to be extremely obsessed with the long term. What do we think we can get accomplished in a decade?" [[4]](https://tylerhogge.com/2024/01/18/what-i-learned-from-alfred-lin-of-sequoia/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
8. **On holding ideas in tension:** Sequoia's culture values holding opposing ideas in tension, such as individual performance and teamwork, or being supportive and demanding at the same time. [[14]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jv4cwqO87eI&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
9. **On market timing:** "To be too early is just as bad as being too late." [[6]](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=SV0KFc4cSxc&pp=ygUSI2NocmlzdGluYWNhY2lvcHBv&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
10. **On the cost of missing opportunities:** "The cost of missing a spectacular opportunity is far greater than the cost of losing money on an opportunity that didn't work out." [[11]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/article/seven-questions-with-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
11. **On long-term holdings:** "We think about whether the company has brighter prospects in the future than they do today. And if that's the case, then we continue to hold." [[8]](https://www.acquired.fm/episodes/special-sequoia-capitals-investment-playbook-with-alfred-lin?ref=antoinebuteau.com)[[15]](https://www.deciphr.ai/podcast/special-sequoia-capitals-investment-playbook-with-alfred-lin?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
12. **On global reach:** "Talent is evenly distributed and opportunity is not... we're sort of expanding around the world because we want to be a global partnership." [[15]](https://www.deciphr.ai/podcast/special-sequoia-capitals-investment-playbook-with-alfred-lin?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
13. **On the investment pace:** Each partner at Sequoia's early-stage team makes only one or two seed or venture investments a year to ensure they can partner closely with founders. [[8]](https://www.acquired.fm/episodes/special-sequoia-capitals-investment-playbook-with-alfred-lin?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
14. **On the nature of the business:** "We work in a business where 30% of the time we strike out, so it's going to happen." [[16]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gf-d-Hrx6_A&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
15. **On building enduring franchises:** "When we invest, we make it clear to the founder(s), we are not interesting in creating a nice lifestyle business. We are looking to build an independent enduring franchise on our way to building a category king." [[7]](https://thoughteconomics.com/sequoia-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)

### **On Business and Strategy**

Lin's broader views on business reflect a focus on the customer and the fundamental drivers of success.

1. **On customer experience:** "I think if companies start reinventing themselves and focus on the customer experience more, they will win out in the end." [[17]](https://www.brainyquote.com/authors/alfred-lin-quotes?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
2. **On the global nature of business:** "Businesses are not just local or even national anymore - good ideas are immediately global." [[17]](https://www.brainyquote.com/authors/alfred-lin-quotes?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
3. **On enduring companies:** "It's harder than ever to build an enduring company." [[17]](https://www.brainyquote.com/authors/alfred-lin-quotes?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
4. **On competition from day one:** "As soon as a product strikes a nerve with customers, competitors emerge globally because the costs to start are so low." [[17]](https://www.brainyquote.com/authors/alfred-lin-quotes?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
5. **On the importance of being different:** "You can't just be better, you have to be different too. You don't want to compete with someone bigger than you head on because they have more money, they have more people, they have more history." [[18]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=91WKEsbHUNc&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
6. **On the power of technology:** Every company will need a strategy around AI and ML, just as they did with the internet, mobile, and the cloud. [[16]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gf-d-Hrx6_A&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
7. **On market opportunities:** "The market opportunities are much larger than we've ever imagined or seen." [[17]](https://www.brainyquote.com/authors/alfred-lin-quotes?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
8. **On the shift in AI:** Lin highlights the shift from mere automation to enhancing creativity and customer experiences through AI. [[5]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PDAcJ-ih4wA&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
9. **On the future of discovery:** "AI assistants are becoming the new front door to every business. If you're not showing up in those conversations, you're invisible to billions of consumers." [[19]](https://pulse2.com/profound-35-million-series-b-raised-for-ai-visibility-and-content-optimization-platform/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)
10. **On the importance of inputs:** "When you want to accomplish something, it's about the inputs. It's about the process by which you go about getting the outputs." [[18]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=91WKEsbHUNc&ref=antoinebuteau.com)
11. **On the long and short view of time:** A friend shared the adage, "the days are long, but the years are short," which Lin applies to focusing on both the immediate moments and long-term milestones. [[11]](https://www.sequoiacap.com/article/seven-questions-with-alfred-lin/?ref=antoinebuteau.com)

# Alfred Lin

* Seed/Early

[View twitter profile](https://www.twitter.com/Alfred_Lin)
[View linkedin profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/linalfred)
[Get in touch with Alfred](mailto:Early-AL@sequoiacap.com)

> Dream about the dent you will put in the universe. Stay grounded in your reality. Build a plan that connects these two worlds.

## Backstory

Emigrating from Taiwan at the age of six, my parents moved me and my brother every few years to better school districts. I was always fascinated by businesses, how they operated and how to invest in them. In junior high, I tried to scale my lawn-mowing service—It didn’t even last a whole summer. In college, I bought whole pizzas and sold them by the slice, but I swear, it wasn’t predatory.

Academically, I had honored my parents’ wishes to be a good student, especially in math and science. I even entered a Ph.D. program in statistics, but I found reading business cases more interesting. When Sequoia invested, I joined my college friend Tony Hsieh at his startup, LinkExchange. After we sold LinkExchange to Microsoft (and made Sequoia 17x in 17 months), we started Venture Frogs, which was both an incubator and a seed fund. Out of that came Tellme Networks, which I joined in 2001, and Zappos, which I joined in 2005.

Michael Moritz had been our Sequoia partner at LinkExchange and we chose to work with him again at Zappos. He was a mentor to me and the rest of the management team. He always showed up, having pored through the board materials and listening intensely to every word. He would focus our attention on the first order issues, sharpen our thinking and push us to be even more ambitious. I learned from him that a partner’s most important value-add comes when the chips are down.

## On the lookout for

All investors essentially look for the same things: (1) outlier founders, (2) a delightful product or service, (3) a path to a massive market, and (4) a disruptive business model. This leads to a large universe of investable opportunities, which is far greater than our capacity to serve on boards and really partner with founders. To have an edge, you have to play the game differently.

I am much more focused on the founders’ special characteristics, their novel and unique insights, the eureka moment of their founding story, and their lived experiences that creates empathy for the customer they will serve. Basically, I am on the lookout for founder-market fit.

After you strip through all that, there is only one criteria for me: would I love to work for the founders and help them reach their and their company’s full potential? It is our job as partners, not just investors—to work for the founders.

# Part II: Alfred Lin, Sequoia

### OpenAI, Airbnb, Citadel Securities, Profound, Clay, Kalshi

## **Founder-Market Fit, Backing Category Creators, Surviving Crises, & Building for the World**

**[Alfred Lin, Partner at Sequoia Capital](https://www.sequoiacap.com/people/alfred-lin/)** and three-time #1 Midas List investor, has backed some of the most transformative companies of the past two decades. His portfolio spans early bets on **Airbnb, DoorDash, Uber, Instacart, Reddit, Zipline, Kalshi, Fireworks AI, Physical Intelligence, & Commure,** to growth-stage partnerships with **OpenAI & Citadel Securities**. Alfred consistently identifyies founders & businesses that redefine entire markets.

### → Listen on [X](https://x.com/MollySOShea/status/1983900491437502703), [Spotify](https://open.spotify.com/episode/2J54zSljCriqIRfaiVDusn?si=ht_cbSa-Q3esnaisHgsPSw), [YouTube](https://youtu.be/Zy2Uvm7_ky8), [Apple](https://apple.co/4oDewfM)

He continues this track record today with next-generation category creators like **Profound**, which is building AI-powered brand visibility, **Nominal,** which is powering mission-critical engineering with a unified data stack,and **Clay**, which evolved into a go-to-market engine by relentlessly adapting to innovation.

In this conversation, Alfred shares his philosophy on founder-market fit, resilience, and backing visionaries who create new categories. He reflects on **Airbnb’s near-collapse during COVID**, **DoorDash’s rise**, and Sequoia’s landmark investment in **Citadel Securities**, while breaking down how he evaluates IPOs, valuations in the AI era, and why the next decade will be defined by reimagined consumer experiences.

## **CEO Peng Zhao & Sequoia’s Alfred Lin Discuss the Future of Markets**

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ac2-!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faf8b9bae-87ba-42ad-a17c-9c1823442157_1896x1202.png)

**[Peng Zhao, CEO of Citadel Securities, and Alfred Lin, Partner at Sequoia Capital, joined a discussion moderated by Jeff Maurone, COO,](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W7B4MtSfm0w)**Technology and Low Latency, on how artificial intelligence, automation, and an obsession with client experience are transforming global markets.

### $1 Billion Investment

**Alfred:** “I joined Sequoia Capital in 2010. So I’ve been there for about 15 years, and I work with a variety of companies, such as **Airbnb, DoorDash,** and **Kalshi** that you saw before, and **Citadel Securities.**

**We were invited to invest $1 billion, not $1 million, but $1 billion,** into Citadel Securities. And, we graciously took it. So I get to work with Peng on this board.”

### How Citadel Securities Prepared for Meme Stock Crisis

**Peng: “During the meme-stock frenzy, we were able to operate through that entire episode with zero seconds of downtime.** And that was actually no easy feat. That was only possible because **we planned for six times the capacity of the previous market peak.**

And why was it six times? Because one, you’re starting off to say, **“I need to have enough capacity for the highest amount of volume of message rate I’ve ever seen.”** That’s your starting point. Now, obviously, **your next market peak is going to be higher than the previous one**. So you **double the whatever the previous peak was.**

But then, we told us, we hold ourselves, I mean, then we thought to ourselves that, **“Hey, I don’t think our competitors is going to spend a tech budget to plan for that kind of market peak and disruption and volume that they need to handle.**

**What if they all go down. And if we’re 35 to 40% of the market and then to triple my capacity yet from there on out.”**

So we literally went through that math and planned for **six times the capacity** from the previous peak capacity that we needed.

And we almost had to use all of it. So a very tax-centric, anticipatory, planning tech roadmap allowed us to be there when our clients and customers needed us the most. And that was the case during each individual one of the market meltdowns & melt-ups that was there during each one of the crises that was in the marketplace.

We think about how do we scale the way we serve our clients during the times of stress, rather than **how much doing now cost us on a day in, day out basis?”**

The future of venture capital is not only investing in companies that can sprint to $100M ARR in their first year. The essence of venture capital has never been about immediate revenue. Yes, we’re in an AI era where some startups can scale revenue at unprecedented speed, but if we only focus on chasing those stories, we risk missing real breakthroughs. Our job as investors is to also provide founders the time to get from strategic value to a cash-flowing business. At Greylock, we’ve seen this pattern again and again. Figma was slow to gain a massive user scale. LinkedIn was dismissed as irrelevant. Roblox was written off as another kids' video game. Each of these was treated as pretty much nothing until suddenly, they were everything. The firms that remember this will be the ones who don’t just fund the obvious $100M ARR hockeysticks, but also focus on the underestimated “nothings” that become “everythings.”

Fundamental breakthroughs that matter for the long haul are practically never built in a year or two. The most impactful plays need time and money. But then they’ll return more to VCs than almost any of the others. In the past, Apple, Amazon, LinkedIn. In the present, Nvidia, SpaceX, Tesla, OpenAI. In the future, fusion, AR glasses, humanized robots, re-programming disease cells, brain to computer interfaces. Changing the future takes place on steep, hard paths.

Competitor analysis is one of the most underrated parts of building a startup. This isn’t about copying anyone, it’s about understanding what users already have, what’s missing, and where you can stand out. I know you might be eager to build fast, but this stage is also your chance to really understand the market you’re entering and see how your product can be different. Every insight you find here will save you time and a few costly mistakes later in design and development. This is why I always take time to analyze what’s already out there before jumping into Figma If you’re about to build and haven’t done this yet, I’m happy to help [#founders](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Ffounders&trk=public_post-text)[#startups](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fstartups&trk=public_post-text)[#earlystage](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fearlystage&trk=public_post-text)

It’s time we celebrate the quiet wins in venture capital. When most people think of venture success, they picture the 100x decacorns heading to IPO - [Databricks](https://www.linkedin.com/company/databricks?trk=public_post-text), [Canva](https://au.linkedin.com/company/canva?trk=public_post-text), [Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stripe?trk=public_post-text), or [Figma](https://www.linkedin.com/company/figma?trk=public_post-text) outcomes. These are the outliers that we strive for, that can return the fund many times over. In reality, more than 90% of venture-backed exits happen via M&A, and when done right these exits can also deliver excellent returns for founders and investors. Earlier in my career, I led a company backed by private equity. In a bit over two years, we exited for 2.2x and a 45% IRR, a decent result by most measures. In venture, we tend to focus only on multiples. A 5x over years is a very solid 50% IRR, but the same 5x over 10 years is only 17% IRR (before accounting for dilution over multiple rounds). LPs ultimately measure success in DPI (cash returned) and IRR achieved compared to market alternatives. Timely M&A can be the difference between a good return and a great one. Of course, some of the best venture outcomes require time. But part of successful investing is recognising when a company will scale into a generational company, and when it’s best served by joining one. In enterprise software, some solutions integrate and scale best in a larger product ecosystem. When they find the right strategic home, everyone wins - founders, acquirers, and LPs. Mergers can also unlock value, combining complementary products, teams or markets to accelerate growth. Yet early-stage strategic deals are often under-served in venture due to lack of M&A focus and smaller deal sizes. That’s why at [Quadri Ventures](https://uk.linkedin.com/company/quadrivc?trk=public_post-text), we’ve made M&A a core capability. We host regular M&A roundtables with founders and our network of more than 300 corporate venture arms, helping them evaluate strategic paths to scale, partnership, and acquisition. Not every company will 100x into a generational business, but that’s not the only path to success. When executed well, M&A can also provide exceptional outcomes for founders and LPs. Many SaaS founders build strong businesses doing $1.5 to $2 million in annual revenue, then get stuck. A common next step is trying to raise $2 million at a $6 million valuation, giving up a big piece of what was built just to chase the venture path. But there’s another option. Sell for $8 to $10 million. Take home $3 to $4 million. Start something new. Not every company needs to be a $100 million or $1 billion story. Some exits are $5 million. Some are $8 million. And that’s still a win. If venture capital doesn’t come through, shutting down isn’t the only path. A smart, clean exit can be. Since 2021, dozens of SaaS founders in emerging markets have reached $2 million ARR, then stalled because investors weren’t funding. Many could have exited at four to five times revenue. Most successful founders aren’t first-timers. They’ve sold before, learned, and leveled up. Sometimes a smaller exit is the smarter move. Not every startup needs to be held onto forever. Once venture capital comes in, the business isn’t fully yours anymore. Sell. Reset. Build again.

The most underrated part of startup building "cash cows". In 15 years of watching and building companies, I’ve realised this...The startups that survive long enough to win usually have a silent profit engine hidden somewhere in the mix. -Take Amazon, AWS began as an internal infrastructure fix. By 2015, it was 8% of revenue but over 60% of operating profit. That “side project” became a trillion-dollar moat. -Epic Games built Fortnite, but the real cash cow was Unreal Engine licensing, used by 1000+ studios and Hollywood productions. -Even Dream11 did agency work in its early days to fund the platform. Small, unglamorous work that created the runway to build something big. Cash cows don’t always match your main narrative, but they buy you time to reach it. The key is to make sure they fund the vision, not distract from it....Because sometimes, the only difference between a pivot and a shutdown…is one reliable cash cow.

VC, Accelerator, Studio: What’s the Difference? When it comes to funding and growing a new business, you have likely heard of Venture Capital (VC) firms, Accelerators, and Venture Studios. While they all exist to help startups succeed, they operate in very different ways. For founders, understanding these distinctions is critical when choosing the right partner. Venture Capital (VC) firms provide capital in exchange for equity. They act as financial investors, offering strategic guidance while leaving day-to-day operations to the founding team. VCs usually invest in later-stage startups that already have traction and a proven product. Their involvement is less hands-on, focusing on board-level decisions and high-value connections. Accelerators are short-term programs designed to fast-track growth. They typically provide a set amount of funding, mentorship, and a structured program, often culminating in a demo day where founders pitch to investors. Accelerators are more hands-on than VCs, but their involvement is time-boxed and temporary. Venture Studios are the most hands-on of all. Rather than simply investing in external ideas, studios generate, build, and launch companies in-house. They act as true co-founders, providing capital, a dedicated team of experts across product, engineering, marketing and growth, and a proven playbook for scaling. From idea validation to launch, studios are deeply involved in execution, systematically reducing risk and increasing the odds of success. In short: - VCs supply capital and connections. - Accelerators offer a structured, time-limited boost. - Venture Studios partner in the trenches, building alongside founders from day one. While VCs and accelerators support external founders, venture studios operate as intimate partners, providing the resources and operational expertise most early-stage startups cannot access alone. Venture Studios vs Accelerators Credit to [Chris Tottman](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/chris-tottman?trk=public_post-text). Follow him for more insights like this. Original post below: ===== Most people think starting a company is like lighting a rocket 🚀 Turns out, there are two ways to do it: 1. Build the baby (venture studios) 2. Fuel the rocket (accelerators) Let’s break both down to their bones. 1. Studios = Company Parents They start with nothing. No ideas. No teams. Just raw space. Imagine a lab where designers, engineers, and CEOs huddle daily. They sweat together. Fight. Pivot. Kill 9/10 ideas before year one. You’re a co-founder, but share control (they take 30-70%). No solo heroics. Studios handle legal, HR, funding. You build. For 3-5 years. Slow bake. Fail? They reassign you. Succeed? They’ve planned your exit since day one. 2. Accelerators = Rocket Mechanics They find rockets already in flight. You need fuel (cash), navigation (mentors), and a launchpad (demo day). 3 months. 5-10% equity. Mentors talk, you do. Your metrics rule. Flatline? You’re out. Network explodes. But fail post-program? They ghost. Success = raising cash. Fast. Studios build legacies. Accelerators bet on speed. One isn’t better. Just different DNA. ===== ♻ Repost to help your network. [Michael Stanchev](https://bg.linkedin.com/in/michaelstanchev?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-name)

Founder & CEO @ REVELION | VC Operating Partner | Working with VCs & Founders to Engineer SaaS Ventures from Conception to Scale with REVAI™

 3mo 

Capital alone doesn’t build companies. Execution does. That’s why more VCs are partnering with venture development studios as operating partners—especially in AI & B2B SaaS. At Revelion, we’ve seen how this partnership model creates a win-win for both founders and investors: 🔹 De-risks VC Investments. Studios accelerate MVP → PMF → GTM validation, reducing the chance of capital burning on misaligned execution. 🔹 Accelerates Time-to-Market. A studio’s product + GTM frameworks help startups move 2–3x faster than solo founding teams. 🔹 Improves Fund Outcomes. VCs backed by studio-built companies see stronger pre-seed → seed graduation rates, higher survival, and faster follow-on funding. 🔹 Pattern Recognition. Studios bring operational playbooks refined across dozens of startups—VCs benefit from repeatable learnings. Our 3-step value framework for VC + studio partnerships: Pipeline Strength. Studios surface vetted, execution-ready startups. Operational Leverage. Studio teams plug into founders to fill gaps (product, GTM, fundraising prep). Portfolio Durability. Better execution = higher PMF odds = stronger ROI for the VC fund. 💡 Takeaway: In 2025, capital is abundant. What’s scarce is execution. Venture studios are becoming the operating backbone that helps VCs turn checks into returns. 👉 VCs: Would you rather back a team solo, or one with a venture studio operating at their side? # Reid Hoffman
